preFix.title: Wycinanki: Polish Layered Papercut
preFix.subtitle: Lowicz-style layered papercut with a base circle and cockerel or flower motifs in contrasting colours
preFix.excerpt: Wycinanki is a Polish folk papercutting tradition. The Lowicz form builds up layers of coloured paper shapes, starting with a large base circle and adding increasingly fine motifs in contrasting colours. This pattern covers the full layered sequence.
preFix.sourceNotes: Polish folk tradition; wycinanki is documented in open-access ethnographic literature. No single PD canonical text.

attrs.body: Sharp blades are safer than blunt ones. Change the blade at the first sign of dulling. Always cut on a self-healing mat. Hold the metal ruler firmly with the non-cutting hand behind the line of cut, fingers at least two finger-widths back from the blade's path.
attrs.heading: Cutting safely

content.text: About wycinanki
The word
wycinanki

simply means "cuttings" in Polish. It began as a domestic craft: shapes cut from paper with sheep shears or scissors and stuck straight onto whitewashed cottage walls. By the mid-nineteenth century, the Lowicz region had its own style, layered circles in bold reds, greens, yellows, and blues.
The
Lowicz style
is a
layered papercut
built up in three to five layers from large to small. This pattern uses a scalpel rather than scissors, which gives cleaner curves and finer internal details.
Colour planning

Traditional Lowicz compositions use high-contrast colour pairs: red and green, black and yellow, dark blue and red. Each layer should contrast strongly with the one below it. Choose your colours before cutting: base circle, second layer, third layer, and any fine-detail top layer.
A minimal starting palette: base in red (15 cm circle), second layer in black, third layer in green, final details in yellow.
Layer 1: The base circle
On your chosen base colour paper, draw a circle approximately 14 to 16 cm in diameter. Use a compass or trace around a circular object.
Cut the circle with the scalpel, rotating the paper rather than changing the blade direction. Keep the blade upright and make a smooth continuous cut.
Fold the circle in half, then in quarters. The fold lines will serve as placement guides when attaching upper layers.
Layer 2: The main motif
The second layer is the dominant motif: a cockerel, a symmetrical flower, or a rooster-and-flower combination. For symmetry, work from a folded half-sheet.

Fold a sheet of your second colour paper in half. Draw one half of the motif shape on the fold: for a flower, draw a petal fan from the fold edge outward; for a cockerel, draw the body silhouette so the back of the bird is along the fold.
Cut through both layers at once. Open the fold: the motif is now symmetrical.
If using a light box, lay the base circle on it and place the second-layer motif on top. Check the position and size before you glue anything.
Apply a thin smear of PVA to the underside of the second-layer motif. Press onto the base circle, centred. Hold flat for 60 seconds. Place under a heavy book to dry.
Layer 3: Contrasting inner shapes
The third layer adds a smaller repeat of the main motif shape in a contrasting colour, placed on top of the second layer.
Cut the same motif shape at 70 to 80 percent of the second-layer size.
Centre it precisely over the second layer so an even border of the second colour shows all around the edge.
Glue with PVA and press flat as before.
Layer 4: Fine detail cuts
The fourth layer adds feather details, leaf fronds, or petal veining. These are small, narrow cuts requiring a fresh 10A blade.
Cut thin strips of your fourth colour paper, 2 to 4 mm wide, into tapered leaf or feather shapes by cutting both edges at a slight curve toward a point.
Add a central slot cut along each shape if you want the traditional vein effect: make a single straight cut from base to tip, stopping 2 mm from the tip.
Arrange these detail pieces radiating outward from the centre of the composition. Glue each with a tiny dot of PVA at the base only, so the tips remain unglued and slightly raised.
Mounting the finished piece

Once all layers are dry, the completed papercut can be mounted on a contrasting backing sheet or glued directly into a journal page. Apply PVA sparingly to the underside of the base circle only. Press under a heavy flat object for at least 30 minutes.

items.fix: Rotate the paper slowly toward the blade as you cut, keeping the blade almost stationary. The paper movement creates the curve.
items.cause: Blade is moving in short incremental strokes rather than a fluid curve
items.symptom: Curved cuts have jagged or stepped edges

items.fix: Apply PVA in a very thin smear using a fingertip and allow it to become slightly tacky before pressing the layers together.
items.cause: PVA was applied too thickly, causing the paper to buckle
items.symptom: Upper layers do not sit flat after gluing

items.fix: Align the paper edges precisely before the fold crease. Press the crease firmly with a bone folder before drawing the motif shape.
items.cause: The fold was not perfectly aligned before cutting
items.symptom: Symmetrical fold cut is not matching on both halves

initialFindings.message: tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
tricolon: tricolon (three parallel items) — two adjectives almost always beats three
historical century reference "nineteenth century" in body — move to sourceNotes or introduce with a person (e.g. "the 17th-century herbalist X")
